items.title: Understanding Calcium Regulation: Why PTH and Vitamin D are Key
items.content: Calcium regulation is crucial for numerous physiological functions, including bone structure, cellular signaling, cardiac function, muscle contractility, immune response, and neural transmission. Serum calcium levels are tightly controlled through feedback mechanisms involving parathyroid hormone (PTH) and vitamin D metabolites 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D] and 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D]. PTH promotes osteoclast-mediated bone resorption via RANKL production to mobilize skeletal calcium.
keyPoints: Calcium is vital for structural integrity of bones as hydroxyapatite ($Ca_{10}(PO_{4})_{6}(OH)_{2}$).
Serum calcium levels are regulated by PTH and vitamin D metabolites 1,25(OH)2D and 25(OH)D.
PTH increases RANKL production to stimulate osteoclast activity for bone resorption.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.title: Clinical Implications of Calcium Disorders: Acute vs. Chronic Effects
items.content: Disorders in serum calcium concentration are common, stemming from primary or secondary disruptions in calciotropic functions. These disorders can lead to acute impacts on organ function and chronic compensatory responses with clinical consequences such as osteoporosis and nephrocalcinosis.
keyPoints: Serum calcium levels are tightly regulated; disturbances result in hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia.
Acute changes affect immediate organ function, while chronic adaptations can lead to conditions like osteoporosis and nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism and Genetic Links in pHPT
items.content: Primary hyperparathyroidism (pHPT) is the most common cause of hypercalcemia, usually due to a parathyroid adenoma or hyperplasia. In about 30% of cases, even nonfamilial sporadic pHPT has identifiable genetic causes affecting cyclin D1 or menin expression.
keyPoints: pHPT is the most common cause of hypercalcemia.
Approximately 30% of nonfamilial sporadic pHPT have genetic causes affecting cyclin D1 or menin.
Reduced calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) expression due to CASR chromatin inhibitory H3K27me3 and H3K9me3 hypermethylation is observed in about 50% of sporadic parathyroid adenomas.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Malignancy-Induced Hypercalcemia via PTHrP
items.content: In advanced malignancies, hypercalcemia is often due to the production of PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) by tumors. PTHrP mimics PTH by binding its receptor, causing similar effects on bone and kidneys.
keyPoints: Advanced malignancy frequently causes hypercalcemia through PTHrP production.
PTHrP shares homology with the first 13 amino acids of PTH and binds to the PTH receptor.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Granulomatous Disorders and Hypercalcemia via Vitamin D Metabolism
items.content: Hypercalcemia in granulomatous diseases like sarcoidosis is due to extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)₂D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity. This leads to increased intestinal calcium absorption and suppressed PTH.
keyPoints: Granulomatous disorders cause hypercalcemia via extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)₂D.
Macrophage CYP27B1 activity in granulomas leads to increased intestinal calcium absorption and suppressed PTH.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Hypercalcemia Symptoms and Severity
items.content: Mild hypercalcemia is often asymptomatic but can present with vague neuropsychiatric symptoms, fatigue, muscle weakness, constipation, hyporeflexia, or nephrolithiasis. Moderate to severe hypercalcemia may lead to lethargy, stupor, coma, gastrointestinal issues, and acute renal failure.
keyPoints: Mild hypercalcemia: 10.5–11.9 mg/dL, often asymptomatic
Moderate to severe hypercalcemia: ≥12–14 mg/dL, may cause lethargy, stupor, coma
Severe hypercalcemia: ≥14 mg/dL, can lead to acute renal failure and significant ECG changes
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.title: Correcting Serum Calcium for Albumin Levels
items.content: When evaluating hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia, correct serum calcium levels based on albumin concentration to avoid pseudohypercalcemia or pseudohypocalcemia. Adjust total calcium by adding or subtracting 0.8 mg/dL per g/dL deviation from the reference albumin level of 4.0 g/dL.
keyPoints: Correct serum calcium for albumin: Add 0.8 mg/dL for each g/dL below 4.0 g/dL
Subtract 0.8 mg/dL for each g/dL above 4.0 g/dL
Approximately 50% of total serum calcium is ionized
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing pHPT from FHH in Hypercalcemia
items.content: In hypercalcemia, measure PTH levels using a two-site assay. Elevated or normal PTH with high calcium and low-normal phosphorus suggests pHPT. A urinary fractional excretion of calcium >0.02 supports this diagnosis. Consider FHH if CaSR function is impaired, indicated by a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio <0.01.
keyPoints: PTH level measurement: Use two-site assay
pHPT indication: Elevated or normal PTH with high calcium and low-normal phosphorus
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH): Calcium/creatinine clearance ratio <0.01
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
items.content: In cases of hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH, malignancy is a likely cause. A PTHrP level may be necessary to confirm hypercalcemia of malignancy.
keyPoints: Suppressed PTH indicates hypercalcemia independent of parathyroid function.
Most common cause in this scenario is malignancy.
PTHrP levels can help diagnose hypercalcemia of malignancy.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.title: Evaluating Multiple Myeloma in Hypercalcemia
items.content: In hypercalcemic patients, multiple myeloma should be considered. Serum free light chains measurement is crucial as ~3% of cases may not be detected by standard electrophoresis.
keyPoints: Multiple myeloma must be evaluated when diagnosing hypercalcemia.
~3% of myeloma cases are undetected by serum or urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation.
Measurement of serum free light chains is important in this context.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.title: Management of Symptomatic Hypercalcemia
items.content: Significant symptomatic hypercalcemia requires immediate intervention to restore normocalcemia, regardless of the underlying cause. Initial therapy includes volume expansion with intravenous saline (4–6 L over 24 hours) to address dehydration and improve renal perfusion and calciuresis. Loop diuretics are used only after normalizing volume status.
keyPoints: Volume expansion is critical for initial management of symptomatic hypercalcemia.
Intravenous saline (4–6 L over 24 hours) is required to restore normocalcemia.
Loop diuretics should not be initiated until the patient's volume status is normalized.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.title: Role of Aminobisphosphonates in Hypercalcemia Treatment
items.content: Aminobisphosphonates, such as zoledronic acid and pamidronate, are used to inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, a major contributor to hypercalcemia. They are administered intravenously with onset of action within 1–2 days, normalizing serum calcium in 60–90% of patients.
keyPoints: Aminobisphosphonates (e.g., zoledronic acid, pamidronate) inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.
Zoledronic acid: 4 mg IV over ~30 min; Pamidronate: 60–90 mg IV over 2–4 hours.
Onset of action is within 1–2 days, with normalization in 60–90% of patients.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.title: Treatment Options for Hypercalcemia Refractory to Aminobisphosphonates
items.content: Denosumab, an antibody to RANKL, is effective in treating hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to aminobisphosphonates. It can be used at lower doses (e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) for patients with advanced kidney disease. Dialysis may be required in severe cases, especially with anuric acute renal failure or congestive heart failure.
keyPoints: Denosumab is effective for hypercalcemia refractory to aminobisphosphonates.
Dosing: 120 mg SC on days 1, 8, 15, and 29, then monthly; lower doses (0.3 mg/kg) for advanced kidney disease.
Dialysis may be necessary in severe cases with anuric acute renal failure or congestive heart failure.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.title: Hypoparathyroidism as a Leading Cause of Severe Hypocalcemia
items.content: Hypoparathyroidism is the primary etiology for severe, debilitating hypocalcemia due to deficient PTH production. This condition can result from various causes including surgical damage, autoimmune disorders, genetic mutations, and other infiltrative diseases.
keyPoints: PTH deficiency leads to profound hypocalcemia
Common cause: inadvertent damage during thyroid or parathyroid surgery
Autoimmune endocrinopathies account for about a quarter of cases
Late-onset idiopathic hypoparathyroidism often involves autoimmunity targeting CaSR and other antigens
Associated with infiltrative diseases like sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, hemochromatosis, Wilson's syndrome, DiGeorge's syndrome, or post-irradiation parathyroid involution
Rare congenital forms due to mutations in GCM2, GATA3, PTH gene, or activating mutations in CaSR/GNA11
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Impaired Intestinal Calcium Absorption and Vitamin D Deficiency as Causes of Hypocalcemia
items.content: Hypocalcemia can also result from impaired intestinal calcium absorption due to severe vitamin D deficiency or resistance, though typically less severe than hypoparathyroidism. This is because compensatory PTH secretion helps buffer the extent of hypocalcemia.
keyPoints: Impaired intestinal calcium absorption and vitamin D deficiency are common causes
Hypocalcemia severity is usually less than in hypoparathyroidism due to compensatory PTH increase
Chronic renal insufficiency can impair 1,25(OH)2D production, contributing to hypocalcemia
Respiratory or metabolic alkalosis increases calcium binding to albumin, causing hypocalcemia
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Signs of Neuromuscular Irritability in Hypocalcemia
items.content: Hypocalcemia can present with neuromuscular irritability, which is characterized by paresthesias and signs such as Chvostek's and Trousseau's. Severe cases may lead to life-threatening complications like seizures and cardiac arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Paresthesias in fingers, toes, circum-oral regions due to increased neuromuscular irritability
Chvostek's sign present in ~10% of normal individuals
Trousseau's sign: carpal spasm induced by inflating a blood pressure cuff 20 mmHg above systolic BP for 3 min
Severe hypocalcemia can cause seizures, carpopedal spasm, bronchospasm, laryngospasm, and QTc prolongation with risk of ventricular tachyarrhythmia (cardiac Torsade) and sudden death
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Hypocalcemia: Assessing PTH Levels
items.content: In hypocalcemia, determining serum calcium, albumin, phosphorus, magnesium, and PTH levels is crucial. A suppressed PTH level indicates impaired secretion (hypoparathyroidism), while elevated PTH suggests secondary hyperparathyroidism due to vitamin D deficiency or intestinal disease.
keyPoints: Measure serum calcium, albumin, phosphorus, magnesium, and PTH
Suppressed PTH with hypocalcemia indicates impaired secretion (hypoparathyroidism)
Elevated PTH suggests secondary hyperparathyroidism due to vitamin D deficiency or primary intestinal disease
Serum 25(OH)D levels assess nutritional vitamin D deficiency
Urinary calcium low in vitamin D deficiency and severe calcium malabsorption
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.title: Management of Acute Symptomatic Hypocalcemia
items.content: Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia requires immediate treatment with intravenous calcium gluconate, followed by continuous infusion and transition to oral medication.
keyPoints: Initial management involves 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate diluted in 50 mL of dextrose or saline, administered over 5–10 minutes.
Continuous IV calcium infusion may start at 1 mg/min elemental calcium for adults.
Monitor with telemetry and serial exams every 4–6 hours.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Chronic Hypocalcemia Treatment in Hypoparathyroidism
items.content: Oral calcium supplements and calcitriol are used to manage chronic hypocalcemia, with careful monitoring to avoid hypercalciuria.
keyPoints: Use 1000–3000 mg/d of elemental calcium orally.
Titrate oral calcitriol (1,25(OH)₂D) to achieve low-normal serum calcium levels.
Monitor urine 24-hour calcium targets: <250 mg for women and <300 mg for men.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Global Considerations in Hypocalcemia Management
items.content: In resource-limited settings, hypoparathyroidism may present with severe complications due to lack of early detection.
keyPoints: Advanced pHPT presents with osteitis fibrosa cystica in areas without routine screening.
Vitamin D deficiency can be common despite sunlight exposure due to dietary and lifestyle factors.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which hormone increases osteocyte production of RANKL to stimulate bone resorption?
items.explanation: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases osteocyte production of RANKL, which stimulates osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.question: What is the primary mineral form in which calcium provides structure to the skeleton?
items.explanation: Calcium provides structural strength to the skeleton as hydroxyapatite, Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.question: Which vitamin D metabolite is involved in regulating serum calcium concentrations?
items.explanation: 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D] is a vitamin D metabolite involved in regulating serum calcium concentrations.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.question: Which genetic mutation is most commonly associated with familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH) type 1?
items.explanation: FHH type 1 is most commonly associated with inactivating mutations in the CASR gene.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: What mechanism leads to hypercalcemia in patients with advanced malignancy?
options: Increased PTH secretion due to parathyroid adenoma
items.explanation: Many solid tumors produce PTH-related peptide (PTHrP), which mimics the effects of PTH and causes hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which condition is characterized by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)₂D causing hypercalcemia?
items.explanation: In granulomatous disorders, macrophage CYP27B1 activity converts 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)₂D, causing hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a common cause of hypercalcemia in patients with prolonged immobilization?
options: Increased PTH secretion due to parathyroid adenoma
Reduced mechanical loading of bone increasing osteoclast activity
items.explanation: Prolonged immobilization leads to reduced mechanical loading of bone, which increases osteocyte RANKL production and osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following symptoms are commonly associated with mild hypercalcemia?
items.explanation: Mild hypercalcemia may present with vague neuropsychiatric symptoms such as trouble concentrating and personality changes.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.question: What is the effect of hypercalcemia on renal concentrating ability?
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia decreases renal concentrating ability in part by down-regulating renal collecting duct aquaporin 2.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.question: How should serum calcium be corrected when albumin concentration is reduced?
options: Subtract 0.8 mg/dL for every decrement of 1.0 g/dL below 4.0 g/dL
Add 0.8 mg/dL for every increment of 1.0 g/dL above 4.0 g/dL
Add 0.8 mg/dL for every decrement of 1.0 g/dL below 4.0 g/dL
items.explanation: When serum albumin concentrations are reduced, add 0.8 mg/dL to the total calcium level for each decrement in serum albumin of 1.0 g/dL below 4.0 g/dL.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of chronic hypercalcemia?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism or familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
Recent initiation of an aromatase inhibitor
items.explanation: Chronic hypercalcemia is most commonly caused by primary hyperparathyroidism (pHPT) or familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH).
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.question: In a patient with hypercalcemia and suppressed PTH levels, which condition is most commonly indicated?
items.explanation: A suppressed PTH level in the face of hypercalcemia most often indicates an underlying malignancy.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.question: Which test may be necessary to confirm hypercalcemia of malignancy when a tumor is suspected?
items.explanation: A PTHrP level may be needed to establish the diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.question: In which condition is measurement of serum 1,25(OH)₂D particularly useful?
items.explanation: Serum 1,25(OH)₂D measurement is useful in hypercalcemia caused by granulomatous disorders.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.question: What is the initial step in managing significant symptomatic hypercalcemia?
options: Begin with volume expansion using intravenous saline.
Initiate treatment with loop diuretics to increase calcium excretion.
Start therapy with calcitonin for rapid reduction in bone resorption.
items.explanation: Volume expansion is the initial step because hypercalcemia leads to dehydration, and saline improves renal perfusion and calciuresis.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.question: Which medication is contraindicated in patients with a glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m² for treating hypercalcemia?
items.explanation: Intravenous aminobisphosphonates like pamidronate are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m².
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.question: Which agent is used to treat hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to aminobisphosphonates?
items.explanation: Denosumab, an antibody to RANKL, is effective in treating hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to aminobisphosphonates.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which glucocorticoids treat vitamin D-mediated hypercalcemia?
options: They increase renal excretion of calcium.
They decrease intestinal absorption of calcium and reduce 1,25(OH)₂D production.
They enhance the action of calcitonin.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids treat vitamin D-mediated hypercalcemia by reducing intestinal calcium absorption and decreasing 1,25(OH)₂D production.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with hypoparathyroidism in adults?
options: Inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands during surgery
items.explanation: Hypoparathyroidism most commonly results from inadvertent damage to the parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid gland surgery.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of impaired PTH secretion secondary to magnesium deficiency?
items.explanation: Impaired PTH secretion can be secondary to magnesium deficiency, which may arise from drug-induced renal loss including chronic alcohol abuse.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which condition is associated with hypocalcemia due to increased calcium binding to albumin?
items.explanation: Both respiratory and metabolic alkalosis can cause hypocalcemia by increasing calcium binding to albumin.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which physical sign is elicited by gentle tapping of the facial nerve in patients with hypocalcemia?
items.explanation: Chvostek's sign is elicited by gentle tapping of the facial nerve in hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.question: What maneuver can induce carpal spasm to diagnose neuromuscular irritability due to hypocalcemia?
options: Inflating an upper arm blood pressure cuff above systolic BP
Performing a deep tendon reflex test
items.explanation: Carpal spasm can be induced by inflating an upper arm blood pressure cuff to diagnose hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.question: Which condition is associated with difficulty rising from a chair while sitting with arms crossed over the chest in patients with profound vitamin D insufficiency?
items.explanation: Proximal myopathy is associated with difficulty rising from a chair in profound vitamin D insufficiency.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.question: Which form of calcium is preferred for supplementation in patients with intestinal disease, due to better absorption?
items.explanation: Calcium citrate is preferred because it is better absorbed and increases urinary citrate.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What is the initial management for acute, symptomatic hypocalcemia?
items.explanation: Acute, symptomatic hypocalcemia is initially managed with intravenous calcium gluconate.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What is the target urine 24-hour calcium level for patients treated with oral calcium and calcitriol for chronic hypoparathyroidism?
options: Under 250 mg in women, under 300 mg in men
Under 350 mg in women, under 400 mg in men
items.explanation: Urine 24-hour calcium targets are under 250 mg for women and under 300 mg for men.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which vitamin D form is typically used to treat nutritional vitamin D deficiency?
items.explanation: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency generally responds to moderate doses of oral ergocalciferol.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Serum calcium concentrations are maintained within a very narrow range through feedback mechanisms.
items.explanation: Feedback mechanisms involving PTH and vitamin D metabolites maintain serum calcium concentrations within a narrow range.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.statement: Chronic compensatory responses to altered serum calcium levels do not have clinical consequences.
items.explanation: Chronic compensatory responses can lead to clinical consequences such as osteoporosis or nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.statement: Hypercalcemia in granulomatous diseases is primarily due to increased PTH secretion.
items.explanation: In granulomatous diseases, hypercalcemia is caused by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)₂D, not increased PTH secretion.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Vitamin D intoxication can cause prolonged hypercalcemia due to the longer half-lives of vitamin D and its metabolites.
items.explanation: Excessive ingestion of vitamin D leads to displacement of 25(OH)D from DBP, causing acute bioavailability that prolongs hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Hypercalcemia can result in significant electrocardiographic changes, including bradycardia and atrioventricular block.
items.explanation: The passage states that hypercalcemia can cause significant ECG changes such as bradycardia and AV block.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.statement: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio of <0.01 is indicative of primary hyperparathyroidism.
items.explanation: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio of <0.01 suggests familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH), not primary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.statement: Measurement of serum free light chains is essential for diagnosing all cases of multiple myeloma.
items.explanation: ~3% of myeloma may be undetected by standard tests, so measurement of serum free light chains can help detect these cases.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.statement: Serum phosphate levels are typically low in vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)₂D-mediated hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: In these conditions, serum phosphate is frequently elevated or high normal.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.statement: Loop diuretics should be initiated before restoring the patient's volume status to normal.
items.explanation: Loop diuretics should not be used until the volume status has been restored to normal.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.statement: Cinacalcet is used to treat hypercalcemia caused by parathyroid carcinoma.
items.explanation: Cinacalcet, a CaSR agonist, is used for treating parathyroid carcinoma-induced hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.statement: Hypoparathyroidism is never associated with autoimmune conditions.
items.explanation: Hypoparathyroidism can be a cardinal feature of autoimmune endocrinopathies.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Severe hypermagnesemia rarely causes symptomatic hypoparathyroidism.
items.explanation: Hypermagnesemia can suppress PTH release and rarely cause symptomatic hypoparathyroidism.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Chvostek's sign is present in ~10% of normal individuals.
items.explanation: Chvostek's sign can be present in about 10% of normal individuals.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.statement: Severe hypocalcemia does not affect the QTc interval.
items.explanation: Severe hypocalcemia can prolong the QTc interval.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

items.statement: PTH (1-84) is currently approved by the FDA for treatment of hypoparathyroidism.
items.explanation: PTH (1-84), also known as Natpara, has been discontinued by the manufacturer.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Vitamin D deficiency is uncommon in countries with extensive sunlight.
items.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency can be paradoxically common in sunny regions due to poor dietary intake and sun avoidance.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Disorders of serum calcium concentration are common.
items.reason: They arise from primary or secondary perturbations in calciotropic functions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why disorders of serum calcium concentration are common.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia

items.assertion: Inappropriate PTH secretion occurs in familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH).
items.reason: FHH involves mutations that impair extracellular calcium sensing, leading to inappropriate PTH secretion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: PTHrP produced by solid tumors causes hypercalcemia by increasing renal calcium reabsorption.
items.reason: PTHrP mimics PTH effects on bone and kidney, leading to increased osteoclast activity and reduced renal calcium excretion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not correctly explain the mechanism of hypercalcemia caused by PTHrP.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: In the setting of elevated calcium and low-normal phosphorus, an increased PTH level is almost always indicative of primary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reason: The hypercalcemia of pHPT is often accompanied by low serum phosphate or frank hypophosphatemia due to the phosphaturic actions of PTH.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why an increased PTH level with these lab findings suggests pHPT.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.assertion: Genetic analysis is not necessary for diagnosing familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia.
items.reason: Sequencing of the genes that control CaSR signaling is commonly performed for definitive diagnosis of FHH.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because genetic analysis is important for diagnosing FHH, as stated in the passage.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 1)

items.assertion: The mnemonic 'VITAMINS TRAP' helps in remembering the differential diagnoses for hypercalcemia.
items.reason: It includes various disorders that can cause hypercalcemia, such as vitamin D intoxication and granulomatous diseases.
items.explanation: 'VITAMINS TRAP' is a mnemonic used to recall different causes of hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (part 2)

items.assertion: Aminobisphosphonates are effective in treating hypercalcemia of malignancy.
items.reason: They inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, which is a major contributor to severe hypercalcemia in most malignancies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.assertion: Intravenous hydration is not necessary for mild hypercalcemia.
items.reason: Mild hypercalcemia does not require immediate therapy beyond maintaining adequate oral hydration.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because intravenous hydration is crucial for significant symptomatic hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypercalcemia

items.assertion: In countries with limited healthcare access, pHPT often presents with severe skeletal complications.
items.reason: This is due to the lack of screening and early detection compared to developed countries where it's often found incidentally.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Climate change reduces the risk of nephrolithiasis in patients with asymptomatic hypercalcemia.
items.reason: Increased temperatures exacerbate dehydration and heat-related illnesses, worsening hypercalcemia effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; climate change increases risks for nephrolithiasis due to hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia · Hypocalcemia | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
